signaturesHeadline: TX independent pres: 113,151 valid sigs for 2024 (1% of prior presidential total); non-primary voters only — number confirmed, but verify cycle recompute
deadlineHeadline: TX deadline: 2nd Monday in May = May 13, 2024; circulate only after March 5 primary — AUTO-LIVE
riskNote: Grounding clean on all 8 fields (every quote verbatim; 113,151 = round-up of 1% of 11,315,056 2020 TX total). Two holds: signatures (mixes the minor-party gubernatorial base into the independent rule) and circulatorEligibility (self-flagged UNKNOWN, an unprovable absence claim). Default strict pending SOS confirmation.
sosInquiries: For an INDEPENDENT presidential candidate's petition under Tex. Elec. Code 192.032, are there any statutory or administrative qualifications on who may circulate the petition (e.g., must the circulator be a registered Texas voter, of a minimum age, or a U.S. resident), or may any person circulate it?

Does the independent presidential candidate's signature requirement of 1% of the most recent presidential vote recompute each presidential cycle off the prior presidential general election total (i.e., for 2028 it will be 1% of the 2024 Texas presidential vote), and is the 113,151 figure specific to the 2024 cycle only?

fields.reliedValue: For the independent presidential path only: at least 1% of the total vote received in Texas by all candidates for president in the most recent presidential general election. For the 2024 cycle this equaled 113,151 valid signatures of registered voters who did NOT vote in either party's presidential primary that year (recomputes each cycle off the prior presidential vote; 1% of the 2020 total of 11,315,056 rounds up to 113,151). No geographic/territory requirement on signers. (Strict scope: the minor/convention-party qualification — 5% statewide nominee threshold or 1% of the most recent gubernatorial vote — is a DIFFERENT path with a DIFFERENT base and does not apply to an independent candidate; do not conflate.)
fields.note: All quotes verbatim. 192.032(d)=1%; SOS=113,151; 192.032(e)=no territory; 192.032(f)/(g)=non-primary voters/invalid if signed on or before primary. Math checks: 1% of 11,315,056 (2020 TX total, confirmed via Wikipedia) = 113,150.56 -> 113,151. HOLD because the draft value bundles the convention/minor-party path (gubernatorial-vote base) into the independent rule, which is out-of-scope for accessPath=independent and could mislead; the 'recomputes each cycle' framing is also a derived gloss not stated verbatim in a citation. Independent number itself is correct.

fields.reliedValue: The application and accompanying petition must be filed with the Secretary of State no later than the second Monday in May of the presidential election year — May 13, 2024 for the 2024 cycle. The petition may not be circulated until after the March 5, 2024 presidential primary; a signature is invalid if signed on or before the date of the primary or by anyone who voted in a presidential primary that year.
fields.note: 192.032(c) 'second Monday in May' verbatim; SOS 'no later than May 13, 2024' and 'circulated beginning after March 5, 2024' verbatim. May 2024 Mondays = 6th, 13th, so 2nd Monday = May 13 (date arithmetic confirmed). 192.032(g) invalidity quote present. Correct and self-executing, no legal interpretation needed.

fields.reliedValue: None. There is no per-congressional-district or per-county distribution requirement for the independent presidential petition; the statute expressly provides that a signer's voter registration need not be in any particular territory, so signatures may be gathered statewide with no geographic spread.
fields.note: 192.032(e) quote 'A petition signer's voter registration is not required to be in any particular territory.' verbatim. Independently confirmed against NASS (no distribution language for the TX independent presidential petition, unlike states such as Virginia which NASS lists with a 400-per-district requirement). Negative claim but directly supported by the express statutory text.

fields.reliedValue: UNKNOWN / not established by retrieval. Secs. 141.064-141.065 prescribe the circulator's DUTIES (point out and read each statement, witness each signature, ascertain dates, verify each signer's registration, and execute a notarized affidavit) but do not by themselves establish that there are NO eligibility limits. Do not represent that 'any person' may circulate; treat circulator qualifications as unconfirmed and assume the petition must be circulated by a competent adult able to witness signatures and swear the notarized affidavit, pending SOS confirmation.
fields.note: Both cited section headers/quotes are verbatim (141.064 'A person circulating a petition must:'; 141.065(a) affidavit clause). But the field's substantive claim is a NEGATIVE/absence assertion ('no statutory provision restricts circulator eligibility') that two cited duty-sections cannot prove — the value even self-flags UNKNOWN. Cannot ground the non-existence of a restriction from these sources; strictest stance is to hold and ask the SOS rather than assert open eligibility.

fields.reliedValue: Notarized circulator affidavit required. Each part of the petition must include an affidavit of the circulator, executed before a person authorized to administer oaths (notarized), stating the circulator pointed out and read each required statement to each signer, witnessed each signature, verified each signer's registration status, and believes each signature genuine. A single notarized affidavit covers all signatures gathered by that circulator if notarization is on or after the date of the last signature obtained.
fields.note: 141.065(a) 'executed before a person authorized to administer oaths in this state' and 141.065(c) single-notarized-affidavit quote both verbatim. Directly on point and applies to the independent presidential petition via 192.032(b)(3)(A)->141.062. No interpretation needed.

fields.reliedValue: paper (wet-signature). Signers must sign in their own handwriting; the circulator must witness each signature in person and a notarized circulator affidavit is required per part. No online/remote or digitized-signature system exists for an independent presidential petition in Texas — the SOS publishes only PDF forms (Application 2-38f, Petition 2-39f).
fields.note: 141.063(b) 'The signature is the only information that is required to appear on the petition in the signer's own handwriting.' verbatim; 141.064(1) read-each-statement quote verbatim; SOS 'Independent Candidate's Petition for President (PDF)' verbatim. Wet-ink/witnessed paper process follows directly from witnessing + per-part notarized affidavit. Correct.

fields.reliedValue: None. Ballot access for an independent presidential candidate is by petition only — there is no filing fee and no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option for this office. Sec. 192.032 requires the application to be accompanied by a qualifying petition and the consent statements, and lists no filing-fee alternative. (The fee-or-petition election available to Texas primary/partisan candidates does not apply to this independent presidential path.)
fields.note: 192.032(b)(3) 'be accompanied by:' quote verbatim — the statutory list of what accompanies the application is exhaustive and contains only the petition and written consents, no fee or fee-in-lieu. SOS independent section (quote verbatim) describes application+petition with no fee. The absence is supported by an exhaustive statutory enumeration rather than mere silence, so 'no fee alternative' is reliable without legal interpretation.

signaturesHeadline: FL independent Pres: 145,040 sigs (1% of last general-election registration; recomputes each cycle)
deadlineHeadline: FL: county petitions to each county SOE by noon July 15 of the presidential year
riskNote: All 7 fields citation-grounded against primary sources (F.S. 103.021 2024/2025 — identical, last amended 2023; DOS Candidate Petition Handbook; DS-DE 18A form; NASS). Statute is current and unchanged through 2025. One source discrepancy on deadline: NASS phrases it as filing "with the department of state no later than July 15th" while the controlling statute requires petitions to each COUNTY Supervisor of Elections by noon July 15, with the SOE certifying counts to DOS by the primary date — strictest operative deadline is noon July 15 to county SOEs. circulatorEligibility held to CONSERVATIVE because "no age/residency/voter-status requirement" is a negative-universal inference beyond what the cited sources prove (they prove only that paying any person is allowed, the Code does not govern collection location, and the petition form has no circulator block).
sosInquiries: For an independent (No Party Affiliation) presidential candidate petition (Form DS-DE 18A), does Florida impose any eligibility requirement on the person who circulates/collects signatures — specifically any minimum age, Florida residency, or voter-registration requirement — given that the form contains no circulator certification block?

fields.reliedValue: 1% of the registered voters of the state, as shown by the Department of State's compilation for the last preceding general election. For the 2024 cycle this equaled 145,040 signatures (recomputes each presidential cycle off the prior general-election registration total). Same 1% formula applies to a minor party not affiliated with a national-convention party; an affiliated minor party instead just files a certificate (no petition).
fields.note: Grounded: F.S. 103.021(3) quote verbatim in statute; '145,040 signatures' and the 1% formula verbatim in the 2024 DOS Candidate Petition Handbook. Independently confirmed: 2024 and 2025 statute texts identical, last amended ch. 2023-120 — no change. Form is DS-DE 18A 'No Party Affiliation' (correct independent path). Minor-party affiliated-vs-not distinction matches 103.021(4)(a)/(b).

fields.reliedValue: No later than noon on July 15 of the presidential election year (July 15, 2024 for the most recent cycle). A separate petition from each county is submitted to that county's Supervisor of Elections by that deadline; the SOE verifies and certifies the count to the Department of State on or before the date of the primary election.
fields.note: Grounded: F.S. 103.021(3) 'no later than noon on July 15' verbatim; handbook deadline table 'July 15, 2024 – President of the United States' verbatim; NASS quote verbatim and in the Florida section. Caveat: NASS simplifies to 'filing with the department of state no later than July 15th' whereas the controlling statute routes petitions to each COUNTY SOE by noon July 15, then SOE certifies to DOS by the primary date. Strictest operative deadline (noon July 15 to county SOE) is what the value relies on — correct.

fields.reliedValue: None. A single statewide threshold (1% of registered voters / 145,040 for 2024); no minimum-per-county or minimum-per-congressional-district quota. Petitions are sorted/submitted county-by-county solely because each signed petition must be filed with the Supervisor of Elections of the county in which the signer is registered (a filing/verification logistic, not a distribution quota).
fields.note: Grounded: F.S. 103.021(3) county-submission clause verbatim; handbook 'Signed petition forms are submitted for verification to the Supervisor of Elections in the county in which the voter is registered' verbatim (normalized). Controlling statute affirmatively defines one statewide 1% threshold and imposes no geographic distribution minimum anywhere in the section — determinable from the text itself, so not an open interpretation.

fields.reliedValue: STRICTEST: Treat circulator eligibility as UNSETTLED. Confirmed from sources: the petition form (DS-DE 18A) contains no circulator/gatherer certification block (only the registered voter signs and dates), a candidate may pay any person to collect petitions, and the Election Code does not govern where signatures may be collected. NOT independently confirmed: that there is no age, residency, or voter-status requirement on circulators. Until the SOS confirms, do not represent to the campaign that circulators are wholly unregulated; rely only on the confirmed facts (no circulator block on the form; paid collection permitted).
fields.note: Grounded: 'Nothing in the Election Code prohibits a candidate from paying any person to collect petitions.' and 'The Election Code does not govern where signatures can be collected.' verbatim in handbook; DS-DE 18A read directly — no circulator/witness block. HELD because the value's blanket 'no age/residency/voter-status requirement' is a negative-universal claim that exceeds what the cited sources prove; retrieval cannot settle the absence of every such restriction. Inquiry sent to SOS.

fields.reliedValue: None on the petition itself. Form DS-DE 18A has no circulator certification, no witness line, and no notary block; the only entries the voter completes are the signature and the date signed. A notary is required only for the separate, optional Undue Burden Oath used to waive the signature-verification fee — not for the petition signatures.
fields.note: Grounded and directly verified: read DS-DE 18A in full — fields are print name, candidate names, year, DOB/voter-reg number, address, 'Signature of Voter' and 'Date Signed (MM/DD/YY) [to be completed by voter]'; no witness/notary/circulator line. Handbook 'The only entries that must be filled in by the voter are the signature and the date.' verbatim. 'The undue burden oath ... must be properly notarized.' verbatim (normalized) — applies only to the fee-waiver oath.

fields.reliedValue: paper — original wet-ink signatures only. Florida invalidates electronic submission and electronic signatures for candidate petitions (Rule 1S-2.045(5)(f)4.): copies, email/electronic submission, and electronic signatures are not valid. No remote/online intake for an independent presidential petition; each form must carry the voter's original signature and be filed on paper with the county SOE. (FL online petition systems cover constitutional-amendment initiatives, not candidate petitions.)
fields.note: Grounded: handbook quote 'the Supervisor of Elections shall not verify a signature on a petition unless it contains the voter's original signature. Thus, copies of petitions, electronic submission (such as email), or petitions with electronic signatures are not valid.' verbatim (normalized), citing Rule 1S-2.045(5)(f)4. Correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: No flat filing fee substitutes for the signatures on the independent presidential path — the 1% petition is the sole route (no qualifying-fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option for President/VP NPA). The only money is a signature-verification fee paid to each county SOE of 10 cents per signature or the actual cost of checking, whichever is less, payable in advance. A candidate who cannot afford it may file a notarized Undue Burden Oath in lieu of the verification fee (this waives the fee, not the signature requirement).
fields.note: Grounded: handbook 'There is a fee of 10 cents per signature or the actual cost of checking such signatures, whichever is less... Petitions cannot be verified unless payment is made in advance or unless an undue burden oath is filed.' verbatim; 'If a candidate cannot pay the signature verification fee without imposing an undue burden... may file an undue burden oath' verbatim (normalized); F.S. 103.021(3) cross-ref to s. 99.097 verbatim. Confirmed there is no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures path for the NPA presidential route.

signaturesHeadline: 45,000 valid signatures (statutory cap; 1% of 2022 gov vote = 59,123, so 45,000 controls). 500 from each of half the CDs. Collect a large buffer.
deadlineHeadline: RECOMPUTE PER CYCLE: file 23 weeks before Election Day (was May 28, 2024). 2028 date NOT yet live — do not rely on the 2024 date.
riskNote: 3 of 7 fields on CONSERVATIVE-HOLD: deadline is a stale 2024 date that must be recomputed for the live cycle; circulator eligibility relies on a court injunction (use NY-registered witnesses to be safe); fee-alternative is an unverified absence claim. Core signature/distribution/capture/witness rules are AUTO-LIVE.
sosInquiries: For an independent presidential nominating petition in the upcoming presidential cycle, what is the exact first and last day to file the petition with the State Board of Elections (the 24-week / 23-week window dates), and the exact deadline for each candidate's certificate of acceptance?

Is there any filing fee, bond, or other alternative to the 45,000-signature independent nominating petition for a presidential candidate, or is ballot access for an independent presidential candidate available by petition only?

Is the Schmidt v. Kosinski permanent injunction (allowing non-registered/non-resident U.S.-citizen petition witnesses to sign the appended statement) still in full effect for the upcoming cycle, and will signatures witnessed under it be accepted without challenge?

fields.reliedValue: 45,000 valid signatures, OR 1% of the total votes (excluding blank/void) cast for Governor at the last gubernatorial election, whichever is less. 45,000 is the operative cap: 1% of the 2022 gubernatorial vote (5,912,286 total) = 59,123, which exceeds 45,000, so 45,000 controls for the 2024 cycle. Collected by an independent body on a single statewide independent nominating petition naming the presidential/VP candidates and the slate of electors. Recomputes each cycle.
fields.note: Citations verbatim at ELN 6-142, SBOE running-president, and ballot-access.org. Independently re-derived: 1% of 5,912,286 (2022 NY gov total, Hochul 3,140,415 + Zeldin 2,762,581 + minor) = 59,122.86 > 45,000, so the 'whichever is less' clause yields 45,000. Confirmed correct and current for 2024; will remain 45,000 unless gubernatorial turnout falls below ~4.5M. Strict guidance: collect a wide buffer (Diane Sare filed 70,000 for the 45,000 requirement).

fields.reliedValue: Statutory rule (ELN 6-158(9)): an independent nominating petition for an office filled at the general election must be filed not earlier than 24 weeks and not later than 23 weeks preceding the election. STRICTEST READING: treat the 23-weeks-before-Election-Day Tuesday as the hard filing deadline. For 2024 that was May 28, 2024; for any future presidential cycle this MUST be recomputed and confirmed with the SBOE before relying on it. Each named candidate (incl. president/VP) must file an acknowledged certificate of acceptance no later than the 3rd day after the last day to file the petition. EL 1-106 weekend/holiday rollover may apply.
fields.note: All citations verbatim (ELN 6-158(9), SBOE running-president acceptance rule, ballot-access.org May 28 corroboration). Date arithmetic independently confirmed: Nov 5, 2024 minus 23 weeks = Tue May 28, 2024 (= 23rd Tuesday preceding); minus 24 weeks = May 21, 2024. HOLD because the value is a PAST 2024-cycle date and the rule recomputes each cycle — the live actionable deadline for the next presidential election is not yet established and must be recomputed/confirmed. Over-comply: do not let a candidate rely on the stale 2024 date.

fields.reliedValue: Yes - a congressional-district distribution requirement applies. At least 500 signatures, OR 1% of enrolled voters (whichever is less), must come from each of one-half of the state's congressional districts (i.e., from at least half of NY's congressional districts). In practice 500 per district controls, since 1% of enrolled voters in any CD far exceeds 500.
fields.note: Verbatim at ELN 6-142(1) and SBOE running-president. Independently confirmed: 1% of enrolled voters per CD vastly exceeds 500, so 500 is the binding per-district minimum across at least half the CDs. Current and correct.

fields.reliedValue: STRICTEST DEFENSIBLE: use witnesses who are duly qualified (registered) New York voters who have not previously signed a petition for another candidate for the same office. (Signers must themselves be NY registered voters qualified to vote for the office who have not signed a designating petition for it.) The Schmidt v. Kosinski permanent injunction (EDNY) expands eligibility to non-registered/non-resident U.S. citizens who sign the special appended statement, and the SBOE currently publishes this expansion - but a campaign should NOT bet ballot access on the injunction; default to registered-NY-voter witnesses.
fields.note: Base rule grounded verbatim at ELN 6-140(1)(b); the Schmidt expansion grounded verbatim at SBOE petition-information and running-president pages (SBOE itself states the witness requirements 'have changed' pursuant to the injunction). HOLD because correctness of the EXPANDED rule hinges on the continued force of a court injunction that retrieval cannot guarantee for a future cycle; the over-comply posture for ballot access is to rely only on the base statutory witness rule. SOS inquiry added to confirm the injunction's continued effect.

fields.reliedValue: Each petition sheet requires a signed Statement of Witness by a qualified witness, accepted for all purposes as the equivalent of an affidavit and carrying perjury penalties (ELN 6-140(1)(b)). In lieu of the witness statement, the sheet may instead bear a notarial acknowledgment signed by a notary public or commissioner of deeds who swore each signer (ELN 6-140(2)). A non-resident/non-registered witness (per Schmidt v. Kosinski) uses a special appended statement instead. No separate notarization is required when a qualified witness signs.
fields.note: Both quotes verbatim in ELN 6-140 (the witness-affidavit-equivalent clause and the 'In lieu of the signed statement of a witness... notary public or commissioner of deeds shall be accepted' clause). Stable statutory text; current and correct. The witness-OR-notary alternative is squarely on the face of the statute.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Petitions must be on paper, signed in ink, and circulated by hand; the State Board of Elections expressly states electronic signatures are not acceptable. No digital/online petition system covers an independent presidential nominating petition in New York.
fields.note: Verbatim at SBOE petition-information ('These forms can be printed and filled out by hand. Electronic signatures are not acceptable.') and ELN 6-140(1)(a) ('Each sheet... shall be signed in ink'). Current and correct; no e-signature path exists.

fields.reliedValue: Treat ballot access as petition-only. Assume NO filing-fee, bond, or other alternative to the 45,000-signature independent nominating petition exists for an independent presidential candidate; do not rely on any fee shortcut. (Confirm absence directly with the SBOE.)
fields.note: The cited running-president quote describes petition CONTENTS, not fees - it does not state 'no fee alternative,' so the value's core assertion ('None') is NOT grounded by the quote provided; it is an absence/negative inference. The conclusion is very likely correct (NY independent presidential access is petition-only with no statutory fee-in-lieu), but a negative cannot be verified verbatim from the supplied citation. HOLD and confirm with SBOE. The strict posture (petition-only) is conservative regardless.

deadlineHeadline: Deadline HOLD: not a fixed Aug 1 — first day of Aug, rolled to next business day (Aug 3 in 2026); confirm per cycle
riskNote: All 8 fields grounded verbatim, but every citation is the 2024 (presidential-cycle) DOS instructions; today is 2026 (a midterm with no presidential race) so the next live cycle is 2028. Substantive rules (5,000 sigs, no distribution, paper-only, fee-plus-sigs, circulator no-residency/no-affidavit) re-confirmed unchanged in the 2026 instructions, but the date-specific deadline is stale and the circulator age/registration qualification is unknown — these two are held.
sosInquiries: For an independent/political-body candidate for President of the United States, what is the exact last day to file statewide nomination papers in the next presidential cycle (2028), given that the deadline is administered as the first day of August rolled to the next business day (e.g., Aug 1 in 2024 but Aug 3 in 2026)?

Other than the eliminated in-state residency and affidavit requirements, does Pennsylvania impose any minimum-age or voter-registration qualification on a circulator of political-body nomination papers?

fields.reliedValue: 5,000 valid signatures of qualified registered electors of Pennsylvania for President (statewide nomination papers under 25 P.S. § 2911 et seq.). The 2% statutory calculation in § 2911(b) is not enforced per Constitution Party of Pa. v. Cortés (E.D. Pa. Feb. 1, 2018); DOS applies a fixed 5,000 for President, U.S. Senator, and Governor, and 2,500 for all other statewide offices.
fields.note: Quote verbatim in NASS PDF (PA section) and in the PB 2024 instructions both narratively ("...except for the offices of President of the United States, United States Senator, or Governor, which require 5,000 signatures") and in the signature table ("President of the United States 5,000"). Re-confirmed UNCHANGED in the active 2026 DOS instructions (same 5,000 language) and independently corroborated by Ballotpedia. Court-order basis stated correctly.

fields.reliedValue: Do NOT treat the deadline as a fixed "August 1." The statutory/consent-decree deadline is the first day of August, administered by DOS and rolled to the next business day when Aug 1 falls on a weekend/holiday: it was August 1, 2024 (Thu) for the 2024 presidential cycle but August 3, 2026 (Mon, because Aug 1, 2026 is a Saturday) for the 2026 cycle. For the next presidential cycle (2028), Aug 1 is a Tuesday, so the operative date is likely Aug 1, 2028 — but this MUST be confirmed against DOS's published 2028 calendar before relied upon. Filing is with the Bureau of Elections / Secretary of the Commonwealth, Room 201 North Office Building, Harrisburg, by 5:00 P.M. on that date. Strictest posture: assume the earliest plausible date (first business day of August), never assume any date later than the first of August is safe.
fields.note: Quotes are grounded verbatim in PB 2024 instructions, but they are 2024-specific (circulation Feb 14–Aug 1, 2024). The draft generalizes this to "August 1 of the election year," which is FALSE: the active 2026 DOS instructions set the deadline to August 3, 2026 (Aug 1 is a Saturday). The deadline recomputes per cycle and is calendar-dependent; correctness for a current/2028 independent presidential candidate cannot be settled by retrieving the 2024 document. Held to strictest interpretation.

fields.reliedValue: None for President. There is no per-congressional-district or per-county minimum for a statewide presidential nomination paper — only the single statewide total of 5,000. Signers are organized by county solely as a clerical matter (each two-sided sheet must contain signers from one county, with that county noted); there is no per-county/per-district minimum. The historical "severe distribution requirement" applied only to statewide STATE offices, not to President.
fields.note: "County of Signers" quote and the 5,000 table entry are verbatim in PB 2024. The 2026 instructions confirm the same county-per-page clerical rule with no per-county minimum for statewide. Ballotpedia citation note 19 independently confirms the distribution requirement attaches to statewide STATE office, not President. No distribution minimum for President is correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: No in-state residency requirement and no circulator affidavit requirement for political-body nomination papers (covers independent/minor-party presidential papers) — both eliminated by amendment to 25 P.S. § 2911(d); the circulator need not be a PA resident. UNKNOWN whether any minimum-age or voter-registration qualification applies to the circulator: DOS instructions specify only that residency and the affidavit were removed and are silent on circulator age/registration. Strictest posture: do not assume any person may circulate; treat circulator age/registration as an open question pending DOS confirmation.
fields.note: "...eliminated the in-state residency requirement and the affidavit requirement for circulators of nomination papers for all political bodies" is verbatim in PB 2024 and re-confirmed verbatim in the 2026 instructions. The grounded claim is correct and current. Held only because the draft itself flags an UNKNOWN (circulator age/registration) that retrieval cannot resolve; an SOS inquiry is added rather than asserting "anyone may circulate."

fields.reliedValue: Circulator: a "Statement of Circulator" must be filled in and signed on every sheet after all signatures are obtained, but the notarized circulator affidavit was eliminated (no notarization of the circulator statement). Candidate: must file a Candidate's Affidavit, normally notarized; under Act 2020-15 (signed Apr 20, 2020) the candidate may instead submit an unsworn Candidate's Declaration along with the affidavit in lieu of notarization.
fields.note: "Every sheet... Statement of Circulator filled in and signed" and the Act 2020-15 unsworn-statement language are verbatim in PB 2024; the candidate notarization/declaration language is verbatim on the live DOS third-party page. All re-confirmed UNCHANGED in the active 2026 instructions. Correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Signatures must be collected on the official two-sided printed nomination paper (legal-size 8.5 x 14, printed 2-sided head-to-head); each signer personally signs with printed name, residence, and date (wet ink). Filing is in person or by mail; no online/digital signature-collection system exists for political-body/presidential nomination papers — DOS provides only blank printable forms.
fields.note: "Nomination papers must be printed 2-sided on 8 1/2 x 14\" paper, head to head" verbatim on the live DOS third-party page; "Each signer must personally sign... printed name, place of residence and the date of signing" verbatim in PB 2024. The 2026 instructions confirm "Pages are double-sided." Paper-only with no e-signature option is correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: $200.00 filing fee for President, required IN ADDITION TO (not in lieu of) the 5,000 signatures — there is no signature-vs-fee alternative; both are required. The fee must accompany the nomination papers, paid by certified check or money order payable to the "Commonwealth of Pennsylvania" (no personal checks or cash).
fields.note: "For Statewide offices (President of the United States...) — $200.00" and the CERTIFIED CHECK/MONEY ORDER payment language are verbatim in PB 2024; NASS confirms "a $200 fee, and nomination papers containing the signatures of 5000 qualified electors." The 2026 instructions and the live DOS page confirm the certified-check/money-order requirement. PA has no fee-vs-signature substitution; fee is additive. Correct and current.

signaturesHeadline: GA president: TARGET ~50,000+ (statutory 1%) — do NOT rely on 2016 court-imposed 7,500 cap
deadlineHeadline: GA petition due 12:00 Noon, 2nd Tue in July (2024 = Jul 9); elector notice due earlier — Fri after 4th Mon in June
riskNote: Two interpretation-driven HOLDs: the 7,500 signature figure is a 2016 "for-2016-only" district-court number whose carry-forward to the next cycle is unconfirmed (over-collect to the statutory 1% to protect access), and circulator eligibility is statute-silent (assume 18+ GA resident/registered voter until SOS confirms). Deadlines, distribution (none), notarization, paper mode, and fee-additivity are grounded and live.
sosInquiries: For the next presidential general election, what is the operative number of valid signatures required on an independent/political-body presidential nomination petition under O.C.G.A. 21-2-170(b) — does the Secretary of State still apply the 7,500-signature figure from the 2016 Green Party of Georgia v. Kemp injunction, or a recomputed 1%-of-registered-voters figure?

Does any State Election Board rule or Secretary of State regulation impose a minimum age, Georgia residency, or voter-registration requirement on a CIRCULATOR of a presidential nomination petition beyond the affidavit/notary requirements stated in O.C.G.A. 21-2-170(d)?

fields.reliedValue: Target the STATUTORY requirement: signatures equal to 1% of registered voters eligible in the last presidential election (historically ~50,334; recompute per cycle). Do NOT rely on the lower 7,500 figure as a safe target — it derives from a 2016 district-court injunction (Green Party of Georgia v. Kemp) that on its face was 'for 2016 only, until the legislature acts.' Over-collect to the 1% level to guarantee access.
fields.note: All three citations grounded verbatim (O.C.G.A. 21-2-170(b) 1% text; Ballot Access News 7,500/50,334/case cite; Ballotpedia statute-never-revised text confirmed as of Nov 20, 2023). The draft's factual narrative is accurate and the 7,500 figure was operative in 2016 and reportedly used in later cycles. BUT correctness as the CURRENT requirement hinges on a legal interpretation retrieval cannot settle: whether a 2016 'for-2016-only' injunction self-extends to the next cycle as the binding number, or whether the SOS would apply a recomputed statutory 1%. Strictest, ballot-access-protective reading: do not bank on the lower 7,500; collect to the statutory 1% (~50,000+). HOLD.

fields.reliedValue: Nomination petition: file with the Secretary of State no earlier than 9:00 A.M. on the fourth Monday in June and no later than 12:00 Noon on the second Tuesday in July preceding the election (O.C.G.A. 21-2-132(e); for 2024 the second Tuesday in July = July 9, 2024). The presidential elector's notice of candidacy must be filed earlier: 9:00 A.M. on the fourth Monday in June through 12:00 Noon on the Friday following the fourth Monday in June (O.C.G.A. 21-2-132(d)(1)) — this earlier deadline is the binding gate. Dates recompute each cycle from these statutory anchors.
fields.note: Both statutory quotes grounded verbatim in O.C.G.A. 21-2-132(e) and (d)(1); Ballotpedia deadline quote also grounded verbatim. Independent re-derivation confirms the two windows and that the elector notice-of-candidacy deadline (Friday after 4th Monday in June) precedes the petition deadline. 2024 second-Tuesday-in-July arithmetic verified (July 2024 Tuesdays: 2, 9, 16). Self-recomputing from fixed statutory anchors; no contested interpretation.

fields.reliedValue: None. No geographic distribution requirement (no per-congressional-district or per-county quota). The statewide total may be gathered anywhere in Georgia. The only sheet rule is administrative: separate petition sheets must be used by signers residing in different counties/municipalities (O.C.G.A. 21-2-170(d)); all signers must be Georgia registered voters.
fields.note: Both quotes grounded verbatim in O.C.G.A. 21-2-170(d) and (b). Independent reading of the full statute confirms there is no per-district/per-county minimum; the 'different sheets per county' clause is sheet-segregation for administrative/verification purposes, not a distribution quota. The 'None' conclusion is a correct, uncontested reading.

fields.reliedValue: Strictest defensible posture: treat circulators as required to be at least 18 years old and Georgia residents (ideally registered Georgia voters) until the Secretary of State confirms otherwise. The statute (O.C.G.A. 21-2-170(d)) imposes no EXPRESS age/residency/registration requirement on the circulator — only a sworn affidavit before a notary stating the circulator's residence address, plus the rule that a notary may not also circulate or sign a sheet he/she notarized. Signers must be registered, eligible Georgia voters. Whether a separate SOS rule adds circulator requirements is UNCONFIRMED.
fields.note: All three quotes grounded verbatim in O.C.G.A. 21-2-170(d) and (b). The draft accurately states the statute is silent on circulator age/residency/registration and itself flags UNKNOWN whether a separate SOS rule imposes more. That admitted regulatory gap is precisely an interpretation/uncertainty retrieval did not close — the bare statute does not exhaust the rules a circulator must satisfy. Conservative, access-protective posture: assume the stricter (18+, GA resident/registered) until SOS confirms. HOLD.

fields.reliedValue: Notarized circulator affidavit REQUIRED on every petition sheet (O.C.G.A. 21-2-170(d)): subscribed and sworn before a notary public, attesting that each signer manually signed with full knowledge of contents, that each signature was signed within 180 days of the last filing day, and that signers are (to affiant's knowledge) registered Georgia electors residing in the county named. A notary who notarizes a sheet may not also circulate or sign that petition; offending sheets are disqualified and rejected.
fields.note: All three quotes grounded verbatim in O.C.G.A. 21-2-170(d). The notarization requirement and the 180-day/manual-signature/notary-conflict provisions are explicit statutory text; correct, current, no contested interpretation.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Wet-ink paper petition sheets with manually signed signatures and a notarized circulator affidavit on each sheet are required (O.C.G.A. 21-2-170(d)). No remote-online or in-person-digitized signature system applies to an independent/political-body presidential nomination petition in Georgia.
fields.note: Both quotes grounded verbatim in O.C.G.A. 21-2-170(d) ('each signer manually signed his or her own name'; per-county separate sheets). Manual signature + per-sheet notarized affidavit are structurally incompatible with electronic collection, and no Georgia e-petition statute covers this petition. 'paper' is correct and uncontested.

fields.reliedValue: A qualifying fee is required IN ADDITION to the signature petition — it is NOT an alternative to signatures. Political body/independent candidates must file a notice of candidacy AND pay the SOS-set qualifying fee (3% of the office's annual salary if salaried; for a non-salaried office a reasonable fee not exceeding 3% of income derived) (O.C.G.A. 21-2-131(a)(2); 21-2-132(d)). The ONLY statutory alternative is in lieu of the FEE: a pauper's affidavit, but only if accompanied by a separate qualifying petition signed by one-fourth of 1% of statewide registered voters (O.C.G.A. 21-2-132(g)-(h)). There is NO fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option — the nomination petition is mandatory regardless of fee payment.
fields.note: All three quotes grounded verbatim (21-2-132(d) fee-and-notice requirement; 21-2-131(a)(2) SOS 3% fee with marked ellipsis bridging verbatim segments; 21-2-132(g) pauper's affidavit). Independent re-derivation confirms the fee is additive to, not a substitute for, the nomination petition, and the pauper's affidavit waives only the FEE (conditioned on its own qualifying petition). The 'fee in addition, no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures' characterization is a correct, uncontested reading of the statute.

signaturesHeadline: 5,000 valid (max 15,000); fixed number, no per-district minimum
deadlineHeadline: 90th day before general (Aug 7, 2024); recomputes each cycle
riskNote: Core petition rule (5,000 sigs, 90-day, paper wet-ink, no notary) is grounded and AUTO-LIVE; two CONSERVATIVE-HOLDs: circulator residency depends on current injunction status, and the "no fee alternative" rests on a statutory gap (president absent from the 3513.10 fee schedule) that needs SOS confirmation.
sosInquiries: For an independent presidential (president/vice-president) nominating petition filed under R.C. 3513.257, is a petition circulator currently required to be an Ohio resident, or does R.C. 3503.06(C)(1)(b) (age 18 only) plus the Citizens in Charge v. Husted injunction mean no residency requirement is enforced for the 2024/2028 cycle?

For an independent candidate for president/vice-president, is any filing fee owed at the time of filing the nominating petition under R.C. 3513.261? The 3513.10(A)/(B) fee schedule and the 3513.10(G) 'statewide office' definition do not list president/vice-president — please confirm the fee amount (if any) and that there is no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures alternative.

fields.reliedValue: 5,000 valid signatures of qualified electors (statewide minimum), with petitions rejected if they purport to contain more than 15,000 signatures. Fixed statutory number, not a percentage; does not recompute by cycle.
fields.note: R.C. 3513.257(A) quote confirmed verbatim on FindLaw ('no less than five thousand qualified electors ... no petition shall be accepted ... more than fifteen thousand signatures'). Ballotpedia quote confirmed verbatim (line 234: 'at least 5,000 qualified voters'). NASS report independently corroborates 'at least 5,000 qualified electors.' Independently re-derived and correct for an independent presidential candidate. Minor caveat: the parenthetical extending the same 5,000 threshold to a 'new/minor-party presidential candidate' is loose (minor-party ballot status runs through a separate party-formation/petition mechanism), but it does not affect the independent-candidate value relied upon.

fields.reliedValue: Not later than 4 p.m. of the 90th day before the general election. For 2024 (general election Nov. 5, 2024) this was August 7, 2024. Statement of candidacy + nominating petition + a sufficient slate of presidential electors must be filed with the Secretary of State by that time. The 90-day rule recomputes each cycle. HB2 (2024 special session) moved only the major-party certification deadline to the 65th day; it did not change the independent-candidate petition deadline.
fields.note: R.C. 3513.257 quote confirmed verbatim ('not later than four p.m. of the ninetieth day before the day of the general election'). NASS report quote confirmed verbatim (lines 846-848) and independently corroborates the 90th-day independent-candidate deadline AND the HB2 note that the 65th-day change applies only to MAJOR-PARTY certification (NASS lines 835-839), exactly as the draft claims. Date arithmetic independently verified: 90 days before Nov 5, 2024 = Aug 7, 2024 (Wednesday). Statute also confirms the presidential-elector slate must be submitted at filing. Value is current and correct; the specific date is cycle-specific and recomputes.

fields.reliedValue: None. The 5,000-signature requirement is a single statewide total; no minimum number of signatures per congressional district or per county. Mechanically, each petition paper must contain signatures of electors of only one county and separate papers are used per county, but no geographic distribution minimum is imposed.
fields.note: R.C. 3513.257(A) and R.C. 3513.261 quotes both confirmed verbatim on FindLaw. Independently re-derived: 3513.257 sets one statewide aggregate (5,000) with no district/county allocation; 3513.261's one-county-per-paper rule is a sorting/validity mechanic, not a distribution quota. Adversarial note worth flagging to staff (does not change the gate): 3513.261 further provides that if a single petition paper mixes counties, the board counts only signatures from the majority county and invalidates the rest — a paper-integrity rule, still not a geographic distribution minimum.

fields.reliedValue: A circulator of a nominating petition for presidential electors must be at least 18 years of age (R.C. 3503.06(C)(1)(b)). STRICTEST POSTURE: also assume the circulator may need to be an Ohio resident — the general residency requirement in R.C. 3503.06(C)(1)(a) is on the books and is reported as non-enforced only by virtue of the Citizens in Charge v. Husted injunction, an enforcement-status fact that retrieval cannot durably confirm as current. Plan for resident, 18+ circulators. Circulators need not be registered voters. Signers must be registered Ohio electors qualified to vote on the candidacy (R.C. 3501.38(A)).
fields.note: R.C. 3503.06(C)(1)(b) quote (age 18, presidential-elector petition) confirmed verbatim; Hamilton County 2024 guide quote confirmed verbatim (lines 47-50, including the Citizens in Charge v. Husted citations); R.C. 3501.38(A) signer-qualification quote confirmed verbatim. The age-18 and signer requirements are AUTO-LIVE-grade. HOLD is driven solely by the residency claim: whether the statutory Ohio-residency requirement is currently unenforced depends on the live status of a federal injunction, which is a currency/legal-interpretation question retrieval cannot settle to the strict standard. Defaulting to the over-comply posture (assume residency may apply) avoids any ballot-access risk.

fields.reliedValue: No notarization required. Each petition paper requires a circulator statement made UNDER PENALTY OF ELECTION FALSIFICATION (not before a notary) attesting to the number of signatures, that the circulator witnessed the affixing of every signature, that all signers were to the best of the circulator's knowledge and belief qualified to sign, and that every signature is genuine. Each petition must also carry the boldface warning that election falsification is a fifth-degree felony.
fields.note: R.C. 3501.38(E)(1) circulator-statement quote and 3501.38(J) boldface-felony-warning quote both confirmed verbatim on FindLaw. R.C. 3513.261's prescribed petition form independently shows the same circulator declaration ('under penalty of election falsification ... witnessed the affixing of every signature') with no notary jurat. Statute uses 'penalty of election falsification' rather than notarization throughout. Value is current and correct; no notary/witness requirement exists.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Signatures must be collected on the official paper nominating petition with signatures affixed in ink or indelible pencil. Ohio provides no remote-online or in-person digital signature system for an independent presidential nominating petition; the petition is wet-ink paper filed with the Secretary of State.
fields.note: R.C. 3513.261 quote ('All signatures on nominating petitions shall be written in ink or indelible pencil') and R.C. 3501.38(B) quote ('Signatures shall be affixed in ink') both confirmed verbatim on FindLaw. Independently re-derived: ink/indelible-pencil wet-signature paper petition, no statutory e-signature alternative for nominating petitions. The specific form name 'Form 3-O' is non-load-bearing color not appearing in the cited statutory quotes, but the substantive paper/wet-ink mode is fully grounded and correct.

fields.reliedValue: Treat ballot access as obtainable only via the 5,000-signature nominating petition; there is no money-payment alternative to gathering signatures for an independent presidential candidate. STRICTEST POSTURE: do not assume a fee can substitute for signatures, and confirm with the SOS whether any nominal filing fee is nonetheless owed at filing under R.C. 3513.261. The 3513.10(A)/(B) fee tables and the 3513.10(G) 'statewide office' definition do not list president/vice-president, creating an interpretive gap as to what (if any) fee 3513.261's cross-reference produces.
fields.note: R.C. 3513.261 fee-cross-reference quote and R.C. 3513.10(G) 'statewide office' definition quote both confirmed verbatim on FindLaw. Adversarial finding: the draft's CONCLUSION (no fee alternative; president absent from the fee schedule) is correct as far as the texts go, but bridging '3513.261 says pay the 3513.10(A)/(B) fee for the office' with '3513.10 lists no fee for president' requires a legal interpretation (does the office owe $0, or is the fee provision simply inapplicable?) that retrieval cannot definitively settle. Because the load-bearing point for ballot access is that signatures are the route (which is safe regardless), HOLD with the over-comply posture and route the fee-amount question to the SOS.

signaturesHeadline: NC: 1.5% of last gubernatorial turnout = 83,874 (2024 cycle, NCSBE-confirmed) + 200 from each of 3 districts. AUTO-LIVE.
deadlineHeadline: NC deadline DISPUTED: statute+NCSBE say noon, day of primary (Mar 2024/2026); Ballotpedia says last Fri June. HOLD on earliest.
riskNote: Two unresolved legal-interpretation conflicts (presidential petition deadline; whether any filing fee applies to an independent presidential candidate) force CONSERVATIVE-HOLD on deadline and feeAlternative; treat the earliest deadline (noon, day of primary) and a possible fee as binding until NCSBE confirms. All citations grounded verbatim; nothing BLOCKED.
sosInquiries: For an unaffiliated/independent candidate for President of the United States under G.S. 163-122, is the petition-filing deadline noon on the day of the primary election (per the NCSBE petitions table and G.S. 163-122(a)(1)), or is there a separate presidential-specific deadline (e.g., the last Friday in June, with presidential-elector certification by the first Friday in August)? Please confirm the controlling date for the current cycle.

Does an unaffiliated/independent candidate for President of the United States owe any filing fee under G.S. 163-122(e)/G.S. 163-107 given that the G.S. 163-107 fee schedule lists no line item for President or presidential electors, or is the office fee-free? If a fee applies, what is the amount and does the G.S. 163-107.1 petition-in-lieu-of-fee alternative apply?

fields.reliedValue: Formula-based: signatures equal to 1.5% of the total number of voters who voted in the most recent general election for Governor, plus at least 200 registered voters from each of three different NC congressional districts. For the 2024 gubernatorial base this equals 83,874 (per NCSBE 2026 Unaffiliated Candidate Fact Sheet). Statewide/presidential path runs through G.S. 163-122(a)(1), with G.S. 163-1(c) confirming an unaffiliated presidential candidate qualifies under 163-122 and nominates electors.
fields.note: All three citations ground verbatim (FindLaw 163-122, NCSBE unaffiliated-petitions table, FindLaw 163-1(c)). Formula is current and correct. Adversarial note: the draft labels the 2024 integer UNKNOWN, but the NCSBE 2026 official fact sheet explicitly states 83,874 (1.5% of 2024 gubernatorial turnout) for the statewide path — the figure IS confirmable from a primary source, so I relied on it. Minor wording variance (statute 'voters who voted' vs NCSBE 'registered voters who voted'; Ballotpedia 'votes cast for governor') is immaterial in practice. Value recomputes each cycle.

fields.reliedValue: STRICTEST: petition signature pages due to the county board of elections by 12:00 noon on the DAY OF THE PRIMARY ELECTION (e.g., noon March 5, 2024; noon March 3, 2026 per NCSBE fact sheet) — the earliest of the conflicting candidate deadlines. County-board verification no later than 15 business days after canvass of the primary. Do NOT rely on any later 'last Friday in June' date until NCSBE confirms in writing.
fields.note: Both citations ground verbatim. UNRESOLVED CONFLICT for the PRESIDENTIAL contest: statute 163-122(a)(1) + NCSBE petitions table + NCSBE 2026 fact sheet all say 'noon on the day of the primary'; Ballotpedia (citing FEC) says the independent-presidential petition is due 'no later than the last Friday in June' with electors certified by 'the first Friday in August.' Reconciling these requires legal interpretation retrieval cannot settle (whether a presidential-specific timeline supersedes 163-122). Strictest = earliest deadline (day of primary), which is what the draft adopts; held rather than auto-lived because missing the true deadline is catastrophic. Note the draft omits the possible first-Friday-in-August presidential-elector certification step.

fields.reliedValue: Geographic distribution required: at least 200 registered voters from EACH of three NC congressional districts (minimum 200 from each of three different districts). Petition pages must additionally be divided into sections by the county in which signatures were obtained, for county-level verification.
fields.note: Citation grounds verbatim in 163-122(a)(1): 'the petition must be signed by at least 200 registered voters from each of three congressional districts in North Carolina. The petitions shall be divided into sections based on the county in which the signatures were obtained.' NCSBE 2026 fact sheet independently confirms '200 registered voters from three different congressional districts.' Adversarial check cleared a stale Ballotpedia phrase ('each of the state's congressional districts') — the statute and the fact sheet both say THREE, so the draft is correct.

fields.reliedValue: NC law regulates SIGNERS, not circulators: each signer must be a qualified registered NC voter residing in the applicable county, and no one may sign on behalf of another. No separate statutory circulator (age/residency/voter-status) requirement found for the unaffiliated petition; circulator-specific eligibility is UNKNOWN (no primary source establishes one).
fields.note: All three citations ground verbatim (NCSBE petitions page signer language; 'No one may sign a petition on behalf of another person'; statute signer requirement). The value is itself conservative — it asserts the signer rules and explicitly marks circulator eligibility UNKNOWN rather than claiming none exists. That is the strict posture, so no stricter reading is needed. Field rests on absence of a circulator statute, which is consistent with the primary sources reviewed.

fields.reliedValue: None / not required. Verification is performed by election officials (Executive Director for statewide petitions, or the county board chair), who check signatures against registration records and attach a signed certificate. No notarized affidavit or circulator certification by the petition gatherer is required by G.S. 163-122 or NCSBE petition instructions.
fields.note: Citation grounds verbatim in 163-122(a)(1)(a): the Executive Director examines names, check-marks qualified/registered signers, and attaches a signed certificate stating signatures were checked against registration records. No circulator affidavit/notary appears anywhere in 163-122, the NCSBE petitions page, or the 2026 fact sheet. Correct and not interpretation-dependent.

fields.reliedValue: paper only. Signatures must be collected on official paper petition sheets, in ink, with original (wet) signatures; electronic signatures are not counted and signatures cannot be collected electronically. No digital/remote-online capture exists for the NC unaffiliated presidential petition.
fields.note: Both quotes ground verbatim on the NCSBE petitions page: 'Signatures cannot be collected electronically.' and 'Signatures must be originals (please use INK); no electronic signatures will be counted. (Photocopies of signed forms are not accepted by county or state elections offices.)' Paper-only is correct and unambiguous.

fields.reliedValue: STRICTEST: do NOT assume the office is fee-free. G.S. 163-122(e) requires an unaffiliated candidate to pay the G.S. 163-107 filing fee for the office OR use the G.S. 163-107.1 petition-in-lieu-of-fee alternative; the NCSBE 2026 fact sheet answers 'Is there a filing fee to run as an unaffiliated candidate? Yes.' Treat a filing fee as potentially due for an independent presidential candidate and budget for the G.S. 163-107.1 fee-waiver petition as a hedge until NCSBE confirms in writing whether President carries a $0 fee.
fields.note: Both citations ground verbatim (163-122(e) fee-or-alternative language; 163-107 fee-schedule preamble). BUT the draft's CONCLUSION ('no fee for President') is an inference — the 163-107 table lists no 'President/presidential electors' line, so the draft reasons the fee is $0. This conflicts with the NCSBE fact sheet's plain 'Yes' that unaffiliated candidates owe a filing fee, and resolving it requires legal interpretation (does the missing line mean $0, or does the Board apply another basis?). Underpaying a required fee can void the filing, so strict posture = assume a fee may apply and confirm. Held, not auto-lived.

signaturesHeadline: Signatures DISPUTED: draft says 30,000; statute (1% of 2022 gov vote) = ~44,620 -- HOLD high
deadlineHeadline: Deadline LIVE: 4:00 p.m. 110th day before general = Thu Jul 18, 2024
riskNote: Only the signature count is unsafe: the draft's flat 30,000 conflicts with its own 1%-formula citation (MCL 168.590b(2)), which on the 2022 gubernatorial total of 4,461,972 yields ~44,620, and Graveline v. Benson (6th Cir. 2021) separately struck the 30,000+distribution+deadline combination for statewide independents -- collect to the highest plausible number (~44,620) and confirm the exact per-cycle figure and Graveline's effect with the Bureau of Elections; all other fields verified live.
sosInquiries: For the 2024 presidential cycle, what is the exact number of valid signatures required for an independent (no-party) presidential qualifying petition under MCL 168.590b -- is it 1% of the 2022 total gubernatorial vote (~44,620), the older 30,000 figure, the Graveline v. Benson interim figure, or another administratively set number?

Does the holding of Graveline v. Benson (6th Cir. 2021) lowering/striking the statewide-independent signature and distribution requirements apply to the independent PRESIDENTIAL qualifying petition, or does the full MCL 168.590b(2)/(4) requirement (1% plus 100 signatures in 1/2 of congressional districts) still govern an independent presidential candidate for 2024?

fields.reliedValue: At least 1% of the total votes cast for all gubernatorial candidates at the last gubernatorial election (MCL 168.590b(2)). For the 2024 cycle the 2022 governor's race totaled 4,461,972 votes, so the strict statutory floor is approximately 44,620 valid signatures -- NOT the flat 30,000 the draft headlines. Plus the distribution requirement (>=100 in >=1/2 of congressional districts). Collect to the higher number and confirm the exact operative figure and Graveline's applicability with the Bureau of Elections before relying.
fields.note: Citations ground verbatim, but the value is self-contradictory and likely understated. The draft headlines 30,000 while its own MCL 168.590b(2) citation states a 1%-of-gubernatorial-vote FORMULA. Independent re-derivation: the 2022 MI governor general election totaled 4,461,972 votes (Whitmer 2,430,505 + Dixon 1,960,635 + others); 1% = 44,620 -- materially above 30,000. The flat 30,000 is the pre-amendment number; the Ballotpedia '30,000' text appears stale. Separately, Graveline v. Benson (6th Cir. 2021) struck the 30,000+distribution+deadline combination for statewide INDEPENDENT candidates (district court set 12,000 interim). The true operative 2024 number (44,620 statutory vs. 30,000 stale vs. 12,000 Graveline interim vs. an administrative figure) is a legal-interpretation question retrieval cannot settle. Strictest defensible posture: collect to the highest statutory floor (~44,620); over-collecting cannot jeopardize ballot access.

fields.reliedValue: 4:00 p.m. on the 110th day before the general November election. For 2024 that is Thursday, July 18, 2024 (110 days before Nov 5, 2024). MCL 168.590c(2). Signatures must be collected within the 180 days immediately before filing (MCL 168.590b(3)).
fields.note: MCL 168.590c(2) text grounded verbatim ('not later than 4 p.m. of the one hundred-tenth day before the general election'); 180-day window grounded in 590b(3); Ballotpedia corroborates. Date arithmetic independently confirmed: Nov 5, 2024 minus 110 days = Thursday, July 18, 2024. Correct, current, no interpretation needed.

fields.reliedValue: Yes. At least 100 registered electors must sign in each of at least one-half (1/2) of the state's congressional districts (MCL 168.590b(4), which expressly lists 'the office of president of the United States').
fields.note: MCL 168.590b(4) grounded verbatim and expressly names president; Ballotpedia corroborates. Adversarial caveat: Graveline v. Benson found the geographic-distribution requirement part of the unconstitutional combination for statewide STATE offices -- whether it survives as applied to independent PRESIDENTIAL is unsettled. But the rule is on the books and complying with it cannot harm ballot access (over-compliance), so it is gated live rather than held. The Graveline question is flagged in the SOS inquiry attached to signaturesRequired.

fields.reliedValue: Circulator must be at least 18 and a U.S. citizen; no Michigan residency required, but a non-resident circulator must mark the designated box on the certificate to accept Michigan jurisdiction (MCL 168.544c(3); HB 5152, 2014, removed residency requirements). Petition signers must be registered/qualified electors of the city/township in the petition heading.
fields.note: MCL 168.544c certificate text grounded verbatim (18+, U.S. citizen, signed in presence), as is the non-resident-box language and the 544c(3) reference to a 'qualifying petition for an office named in section 590b(4)' (which lists president). Ballotpedia confirms HB 5152 removed all circulator residency requirements. Correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: No notarization of the petition. Each sheet carries a CERTIFICATE OF CIRCULATOR self-attestation (false statement = misdemeanor); the circulator must sign and date it before filing (MCL 168.544c(5)); it is not sworn before a notary. (The candidate's separate Affidavit of Identity includes a campaign-finance statement signed before a notary -- a distinct document, not the petition.)
fields.note: MCL 168.544c(5) sign-and-date-before-filing rule and the misdemeanor warning grounded verbatim; Ballotpedia confirms the AOI campaign-finance statement is the notarized component (a separate document). No petition notarization required. Correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Wet-ink paper petition sheets signed in the circulator's physical presence; no remote/online or digitized signature path for an independent presidential qualifying petition in Michigan.
fields.note: MCL 168.544c 'each signature on the petition was signed in his or her presence' grounded verbatim; Ballotpedia signer-address requirement grounded. No statutory authorization for electronic collection located. Paper-only is correct.

fields.reliedValue: None for the independent (no-party) presidential qualifying-petition path -- access is by petition only; no filing fee may substitute. (The $100 fee-in-lieu-of-petition exists only for major-party state LEGISLATIVE nominating petitions.)
fields.note: Ballotpedia grounded verbatim: '$100 in lieu of filing a nominating petition' applies to a major party state legislative candidate only; the independent path requires an affidavit of identity plus a qualifying petition with no fee alternative. Sore-loser provision separately confirms an independent cannot use a partisan path/fee. Correct.

fields.reliedValue: Materially different. A new party qualifies under MCL 168.685: petitions equal to not less than 1% of the total votes cast for all gubernatorial candidates at the last gubernatorial election, same distribution rule (>=100 in >=1/2 of congressional districts), same 4 p.m. 110th-day deadline, signatures within 180 days; chairperson and secretary file a signed certificate with the petitions, and the party nominates its presidential candidate by convention. Same circulator-certificate/paper mechanics (685(4) incorporates the 544c form).
fields.note: MCL 168.685(1) long quote grounded verbatim (1% formula, 100-in-1/2-districts distribution, 110-day deadline, 180-day window, certificate filing) and 685(4) 'balance...as set forth in section 544c' grounded verbatim. Correct and current. Note: 685's use of the SAME 1% formula corroborates that the independent statewide signature requirement is the 1% formula (~44,620), reinforcing the doubt on the signaturesRequired '30,000' figure.
